text: Is there any platform/service out there that connects pair programmers to your editor/IDE session to work on whatever you’re working on with you, on-demand in real-time? Something in between Kite (one example of a service that provides ML-powered code completion in your IDE) and hiring a consultant / person / firm (perhaps from Upwork or some similar platform), something that abstracts over who is doing the pair programming work and makes payments easy?

elements.text: Is there any platform/service out there that connects pair programmers to your editor/IDE session to work on whatever you’re working on with you, on-demand in real-time? text:
> But when I talk to "normal people" they don't seem to feel the need.
In fact, I guess that depends on end-users to whom you are asking the question.

And of course we need end-user programming not for everyone, but for those end-users that have the need and/or the pleasure to do some programming without being an expert.

Maybe you should ask this question to the "NoCode programmers". There are lots of people there who are not professional programmers, but create things with these "programming" environment that are useful to them.

elements.text: But when I talk to "normal people" they don't seem to feel the need.

In fact, I guess that depends on end-users to whom you are asking the question.

And of course we need end-user programming not for everyone, but for those end-users that have the need and/or the pleasure to do some programming without being an expert.

text:
And as already noticed in this thread, end-user programming also means that professional programmers can modify more easily the app they are using.

With the mail client example which is perfect: I still miss lots of simple features from my mail client and even if in theory I can take its code and modify it is still too complicated and time consuming to even add a simple button that will move this mail into this folder.

text: Thanks for that, hadn’t found it before. That’s the closest thing yet. I’m thinking of something that functions more like an IDE plugin (that happens to have a pool of humans involved) than a marketplace for experts. Essentially, developer tooling that’s powered not by traditional software, nor by artificial intelligence, but by human intelligence.

elements.text: Thanks for that, hadn’t found it before. That’s the closest thing yet. I’m thinking of something that functions more like an IDE plugin (that happens to have a pool of humans involved) than a marketplace for experts. Essentially, developer tooling that’s powered not by traditional software, nor by artificial intelligence, but by human intelligence.

text: This IDE plugin would have a lot of latency. How do you imagine the developer experience?

elements.text: This IDE plugin would have a lot of latency. How do you imagine the developer experience?

text: Developer continues coding as normal, pair programmer(s) get to tag along and see how they can help (spotting bugs, suggesting refactors, filling in subroutines perhaps suggested by comments, adding documentation/tests, generally writing code in their areas of expertise to assist the likely more generalist main developer). Perhaps some interface for accepting/rejecting suggestions, maybe allowing some pair programmers to make direct edits, probably some text chat, but probably not video chat. Getting extra sets of hands/eyes/domain expertise during your normal development process.

elements.text: Developer continues coding as normal, pair programmer(s) get to tag along and see how they can help (spotting bugs, suggesting refactors, filling in subroutines perhaps suggested by comments, adding documentation/tests, generally writing code in their areas of expertise to assist the likely more generalist main developer). Perhaps some interface for accepting/rejecting suggestions, maybe allowing some pair programmers to make direct edits, probably some text chat, but probably not video chat. Getting extra sets of hands/eyes/domain expertise during your normal development process.

text:
Ok I see.

Does the peanut gallery only see what the dev sees, or do they get ready access to the whole project in their IDE? Either could be interesting.

elements.text:
Ok I see.

Does the peanut gallery only see what the dev sees, or do they get ready access to the whole project in their IDE? Either could be interesting.

text: I think you have to be a bit flexible about that, perhaps defaulting to only limited access (explicitly shared files or subsections of files), with broader access allowable (built up trust in specific collaborators, non-sensitive or open source codebases, IP agreements signed, etc.). If the collaborators don’t have access to more of the project, they can’t write/reference tests in a separate file, look up definitions, etc., but they can still do many useful things.

text: Outside of the developer productivity angle, is there anything like this for collaborative livecoding on Twitch streams, for example?

elements.text: Outside of the developer productivity angle, is there anything like this for collaborative livecoding on Twitch streams, for example?

text: Looks like you can only get that with some sort of jury-rigged setup today: <https://www.tedmyoung.com/remote-pair-programming-on-twitch-with-zoom>

elements.text: Looks like you can only get that with some sort of jury-rigged setup today:

text: Going beyond one or a couple collaborators with existing pair programming tools (that you then stream on Twitch), I’m wondering how you would get a lot of people in there making coding suggestions. Like, Twitch Plays coding?
